statement: Samtök iðnaðarins (SI, Confederation of Icelandic Enterprise) conducted a membership survey in February/March 2026 on attitudes toward Iceland's potential EU membership. The survey found that 57% of SI members were opposed to EU membership (þar af 43% mjög andvígir — of which 43% strongly opposed), while a minority supported membership. The proportion of members reporting strong opposition had approximately doubled compared to a comparable survey conducted in 2024. SI is Iceland's largest employers' organisation, representing companies across manufacturing, technology, services, and other sectors. The organisation has historically taken a cautious position on EU membership, emphasising both the potential benefits of single market access and the risks of EU regulation, particularly for Iceland's specific economic conditions. SI's membership includes both exporters (who might benefit from closer EU integration) and domestically oriented businesses (who might face increased competition).
caveats: Employer organisation surveys reflect the views of business owners and managers, not the general public or workers. The doubling of strong opposition may reflect sampling variation, changes in SI membership composition, or genuine shifts in business sentiment. The survey methodology (sample size, response rate, question wording) has not been independently verified. SI's public position on EU membership is more nuanced than the survey results might suggest — the organisation has called for thorough economic analysis before any decision.

related_entries.statement: Samtök atvinnulífsins (SA, Confederation of Icelandic Enterprise) has broadly supported EU membership, arguing it would reduce trade barriers, lower the cost of capital, and provide a more stable regulatory environment. Alþýðusamband Íslands (ASÍ, Icelandic Confederation of Labour) has been more cautious, supporting the referendum process but not formally endorsing membership. ASÍ's concerns centre on whether EU labour market rules would weaken the Icelandic collective bargaining model, which achieves high union density (~90%) and strong wage outcomes.

Landssamband íslenskra útvegsmanna (LÍÚ, Federation of Icelandic Fishing Vessel Owners) and Samtök fyrirtækja í sjávarútvegi (SFS, Iceland Ocean Cluster / Fisheries Association) strongly oppose EU membership. Their primary concern is that Iceland's individual transferable quota (ITQ) fisheries management system and exclusive control over the 200-nautical-mile exclusive economic zone would be subordinated to the EU's Common Fisheries Policy (CFP). The fishing industry contributes approximately 8% of GDP directly and ~25% when upstream and downstream activities are included.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Skoðanakönnun Samtaka iðnaðarins sýnir að andstaða við ESB-aðild er að aukast og meirihluti félagsmanna vill ekki ganga í ESB.

citations.report_title: Iðnaðurinn forherðist í andstöðu gegn ESB

claims.claim_text: Vaxandi meirihluti félagsmanna Samtaka iðnaðarins er andvígur ESB-aðild Íslands.
Aðeins um fjórðungur félagsmanna Samtaka iðnaðarins er hlynnt ESB-aðild samkvæmt nýrri könnun.
Könnunin meðal félagsmanna SI var gerð í febrúar og mars 2026.
Fimmtíu og sjö prósent félagsmanna SI sögðust andvíg ESB-aðild Íslands.
Af þeim 57% sem voru andvígir voru 43 prósent mjög andvíg ESB-aðild.
Tæplega tvöfalt fleiri félagsmenn SI eru nú mjög andvígir ESB-aðild en í sambærilegri könnun árið 2024.
Samtök iðnaðarins hafa ekki tekið formlega afstöðu til ESB-aðildar Íslands.
Meirihluti félagsmanna Samtaka iðnaðarins er andvígur ESB-aðild þótt samtökin sjálf hafi ekki tekið formlega afstöðu.
